publisher.publisher-name: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

title-group.article-title: Evaluation of improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush in dogs with caval syndrome

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Veterinary Medical Imaging, College of Veterinary Medicine, Jeonbuk National University, Iksan 54596, Korea.
3Bundang Leaders Animal Medical Center, Seongnam 13636, Korea.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: Hakyoung Yoon. Department of Veterinary Medical Imaging, College of Veterinary Medicine, Jeonbuk National University, 79 Gobong-ro, Iksan 54596, Korea. hyyoon@jbnu.ac.kr

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2023 The Korean Society of Veterinary Science
permissions.copyright-holder: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Heartworm infection in dogs is caused by Dirofilaria immitis and common in shelter animals and outdoors dogs. Caval syndrome can develop with severe infection and physical heartworm removal is essential with heartworm burdens. In this study, we used an improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush, which was expected to cause less cardiovascular damage and allow easier manipulation.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: This study aims to evaluate efficacy of this improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The brush was designed to improve upon the limitations of the previous brush-type devices. The brush was made of a polyvinyl chloride tube and threads of polyamides or polyglyconates. Metal material was inserted at the front tip for easy visualization under fluoroscopy. The eight dogs diagnosed with caval syndrome with large numbers of heartworms and pulmonary hypertension were used in this study. The removal procedure began with the dissection of the subcutaneous tissue around the right jugular vein. The device was inserted through the jugular vein. After insertion, the tube was rotated to catch the heartworms and extracted with the heartworms hanging on the threads. The procedure was repeated several times. Lastly, jugular vein and skin sutures were made. Adulticidal therapy was administered after heartworm removal.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The mean number of removed heartworms was 10.5 ± 4.24 and mean number of remaining heartworms was 0.63 ± 1.06. Total procedure time was 72.63 ± 51.36. Except for three cases, heartworms were not detected on ultrasonography after the procedure. No procedure-related side effects were observed within the 1- to 2-mon.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: An improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush is efficient for heartworm removal in dogs with caval syndrome.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Heartworm infection in dogs is caused by Dirofilaria immititis from various species of mosquitoes [12345]. Canine heartworm infection can be effectively prevented by administration of monthly preventive medications [12346]. However, due to poor prevention for shelter dogs, they have a much higher heartworm infection prevalence than owned dogs [56].

Heartworms reside preferentially in the pulmonary arteries, occasionally migrate into the main pulmonary artery (MPA), the right heart, and even into the vena cava [123]. Due to heartworm-induced pathophysiological changes, pulmonary hypertension, right heart failure, eosinophilic pneumonia, eosinophilic granulomatosis, pulmonary thromboembolism, and caval syndrome can occur [1237].

Caval syndrome is a severe complication associated with heartworms migrating into the right heart, and vena cava, commonly related to heavy worm burden [12389101112]. Migration of heartworms cause partial venous inflow obstruction to the right heart which cause tricuspid regurgitation and tricuspid insufficiency [12389101112]. This leads to low cardiac output and right heart failure acting with existing pulmonary hypertension [1238101112]. Hemolytic anemia can also occur due to red blood cell trauma [12391112]. Thus, life-threatening cardiovascular shock complicated with disseminated intravascular coagulation can occur in caval syndrome and has poor prognosis without aggressive treatment [12381112]. Adulticidal therapy is not recommended for caval syndrome because of the pulmonary thromboembolism and immune response in the pulmonary vasculature from killing the heartworms [1231314]. Therefore, to provide proper treatment and to reduce post-adulticidal complications, physical removal of heartworms through thoracotomy or venotomy is needed [12101112131415]. Due to the high mortality and invasiveness of surgical removal through thoracotomy, interventional removal through transvenous extraction has currently been used [1111213141516].

There are various devices used for the interventional heartworm removal such as forceps, snare, and basket devices [1112131415161718]. These all have the advantages of causing minimal cardiovascular damage and reducing time compared with thoracotomy [811131619]. However, forceps are too large for small dogs and still pose a risk of damage to the cardiovascular tissues [11121320]. Basket and snare type devices can be applied in various size of patients, but cost of the equipment is expensive due to the need of guiding catheter [111213]. Therefore, development of more simplified brush-type devices has been reported recently. They have the advantages of low cost while maintaining the minimal invasiveness. However, depending on the device, some are considered to have difficulties in handling and location estimation, and expected to be somewhat damaging at times [1112162021].

For these reasons, an improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush was devised. The device has been designed to cause less damage because the knot is made in the inner space of the brush. The metal tip also makes it easy to specify the location of the brush under the fluoroscopy. To increase operation convenience, a stopper has been installed to act as a handle. This study evaluated the efficacy of this devised extraction brush as a device for heartworm removal in dogs with caval syndrome.

sec.title: Animals
sec.p: This is a retrospective, multi-center study. The medical records of eight dogs who visited Leaders Animal Medical Center, Allimals Animal Medical Center, and Western Animal Medical Center from August 2019 to April 2022 and who underwent transvenous interventional heartworm removal were included in this study. There were five males and three females; mean body weight 11.52 (2.82–33) kg, mean age was 5 (3–7) yr. All patients were diagnosed with heartworm infection using an antigen test (SNAP 4Dx Plus Test; IDEXX Laboratories, USA). Microscopic examination indicated microfilaria in three dogs. All eight dogs were confirmed to have caval syndrome by the detection of the large worm burdens in the right side of heart, MPA, or caudal vena cava by echocardiography (Fig. 1). Their clinical signs were dyspnea in seven dogs, lethargy in six dogs, and anorexia in three dogs. All eight dogs were suspected to have pulmonary hypertension with moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary regurgitation, dilation of right atrium and right ventricle, or pulmonary artery dilation; six had right-sided heart failure with ascites and one had additional pleural effusion (Fig. 2).

caption.title: Ultrasonographic images of heartworm infection in case 1, 2, and 5. (A-C) Echocardiographic and abdominal ultrasonographic images before heartworm removal. (D-F) Echocardiographic images after heartworm removal. Heartworms detected in the RA, RV, MPA and CVC are presented by hyperechoic double lines (white arrow) in all images. (A, D) Images of case 1. (B, E) Images of case 2. (C, F) Images of case 5. (D) After heartworm removal, there are no detectable heartworms in the RA and RV in case 1. (E) After heartworm removal, heartworm was detected in MPA in case 2. (F) After heartworm removal, no heartworm detected in CVC, but in MPA in case 5.
caption.p: RA, right atrium; RV, right ventricle; MPA, main pulmonary artery; CVC, caudal vena cava.

caption.title: Ultrasonographic images of heartworm infection in case 3, 4, 6, 7, and 8. (A) Right parasternal short axis view of case 7. MPA is severely dilated. (B) Right parasternal short axis view of case 6. RV is severely dilated and IVS flattening is presented. (C) Left apical 4 chamber view of case 4. Tricuspid regurgitation (white arrow) presented and velocity of regurgitant flow was 4.7 m/s. (D) Right parasternal short axis view of case 3. With pulmonary hypertension, pulmonary regurgitation (hollow arrow) also presented. (E) Image of case 4. Most of dogs had peritoneal effusion in abdominal cavity. (F) In case 8, additional pleural effusion also presented.
caption.p: AO, aorta; MPA, main pulmonary artery; RV, right ventricle; IVS, interventricular septum; LV, left ventricle; F, fluid.

sec.p: This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Jeonbuk National University (Approval No. NON 2022-030-002).

sec.title: Device
sec.p: An improved transvenous extraction brush (Able Implant & Instrument, Korea) (Fig. 3) was made with a 45 cm polyvinyl chloride tube. The front part of the tube consists of several strands of polyamides or polyglyconates as threads. The length of the brushes or threads are 3 cm, 5 cm, and 7 cm, and can be selected by surgeons to their convenience or according to the patient’s body size. At the front tip of the tube, metallic material was inserted to facilitate visualization under the fluoroscopy (Fig. 4). At the other end of the tube, movable plastic stopper has been attached and can be used as both a handle and indicator of how long the tube should be inserted into the heart.

caption.title: (A) Image of improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush. (B) Schematic illustration of the brush. The device is composed of tube, threads, metallic tip, and plastic stopper.
Fluoroscopic image of the extraction brush. The tip of the brush (white arrow) is visualized well under fluoroscopy to easily notice in which anatomical structure the brush is inserted.

sec.title: Heartworm removal procedure
sec.p: Prior to the procedure, dalteparin (Fragmin Inj.; Pfizer, USA) (150 IU/kg subcutaneous) and clopidogrel (Lopirel Tab.; CMG Pharma, Korea) (4 mg/kg per oral) was administered to minimize the complications related to coagulation. Prior to anesthesia for heartworm extraction, the dogs were premedicated with cefazolin (Cefazolin Inj.; Chongkundang Pharma, Korea) (22 mg/kg intravenous [IV]), midazolam (Midazolam Inj.; Bukwang Pharma, Korea) and butophanol (Butophan Inj.; Myung Noon Pharma, Korea). Anesthesia was induced with propofol (Anepol; Hana Pharma, Korea) (3–6 mg/kg IV) and tracheal intubation was performed. Anesthesia was maintained with isoflurane (Foran solution; Choongwae Pharma, Korea) or sevoflurane (Sevofran; Hana Pharma) with 100% oxygen.

Heartworm removal procedure was performed in the following order (Fig. 5). The patient was positioned in left lateral recumbency. The skin around the right jugular vein was incised and the surrounding subcutaneous tissue was bluntly dissected. After dissection, the jugular vein was retracted gently. A small incision was then made in the retracted jugular vein to allow the device to enter. An extraction brush was inserted through the right jugular vein under fluoroscopy into the right side of the heart. The brush device was rotated several times to catch the heartworms by the threads. The device was extracted with captured heartworms after several rotations. This procedure was repeated until no worms were captured, and then repeated several more times after the last worm was removed. The jugular vein was then sutured with absorbable suture materials (Maxon 6-0 or Maxon 5-0 or PDS 4-0) using vertical suture method. Subcutaneous tissue was sutured using a simple continuous suture method with PDS 4-0 and the skin was sutured using a simple interrupted suture method with Nylon 3-0. When the skin suture was finished, the entire procedure was completed.

caption.title: Procedure of transvenous heartworm removal using the extraction brush. (A) The right jugular vein is retracted after the blunt dissection of the subcutaneous tissue. (B) The device is inserted through the incision site of the retracted vein. (C) The heartworms are captured in the threads (white arrow). (D) After heartworm removal, the jugular vein, subcutaneous tissue and skin are sutured backwards; this figure panel shows the incision of the subcutaneous tissue.

sec.title: Evaluation
sec.p: The efficacy of the extraction brush was evaluated both quantitatively and qualitatively. For quantitative evaluation, the total number of removed heartworms, number of remaining heartworms, mean number of removed worms per attempt, number of attempts, total number of attempts, procedure time, and total procedure times were recorded. Number of remaining heartworms was defined as the number of heartworms confirmed after the removal through echocardiography. Regarding the number of attempts, this was defined as the number of attempts until the last heartworm was removed. The total number of attempts was also recorded which several attempts repeated after the last heartworm came out were added. The time until the last worm was extracted was calculated from propofol induction to the end of the attempts after the last worm was removed. Total procedure time from propofol induction to the end of the skin suture was recorded. For qualitative evaluation, the visualization of the brush tip under fluoroscopy and ease of manipulation were scored by the surgeons. The scoring criteria are presented in Table 1. In all cases, follow-up of any remaining heartworms was performed using echocardiography (Fig. 1). In addition, the patients were monitored for 1 to 2 mon for any side effects that may have occurred related to heartworm removal.

caption.title: Scoring criteria for quantitative evaluation categories

tr.td: 0 = no visualization under the fluoroscopy during procedure
1 = almost no visualization under the fluoroscopy during procedure
2 = partially defined under the fluoroscopy during procedure
3 = well defined under the fluoroscopy during procedure

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: A total of eight dogs with caval syndrome who underwent interventional transvenous heartworm removal using an improved heartworm extraction brush were analyzed. The breed, sex, age, and body weight of each patient are summarized in Table 2. All dogs except two dogs (case 6 and 7) were small to medium breeds; Pomeranian (n = 1), Poodle (n = 3), mixed-breed (n = 1), Cocker Spaniel (n = 1), Golden Retriever (n = 1), and Labrador Retriever (n = 1). The sex distribution: castrated males (n = 3), intact males (n = 2), and intact females (n = 3).

caption.title: Signalment (case number, breed, sex, age and body weight) of each patient

sec.p: The results of the quantitative and qualitative categories are summarized in Supplementary Table 1. The number of heartworms removed in each trial is listed in Supplementary Table 2. The total number of removed heartworms was 10.5 ± 4.24. Number of remaining heartworms was 0.63 ± 1.06. The remaining heartworms after extraction in the MPA in case 2 and 5 and in the MPA and right atrium in case 8 were confirmed by ultrasound. The number of attempts until the last heartworm was removed was 6.13 ± 7.7, and total number of attempts including the several attempts repeated after the last heartworm was removed was 13.88 ± 9.64. In case 7, five times more attempts than other cases were needed to remove the last heartworm. In other cases, the last heartworm came out within the five attempts. The total mean procedure time was 72.63 ± 51.36 min and procedure time until the last worm came out were 38.6 ± 43.28 min.

In qualitative categories, visualization and ease of manipulation scores were both high. The mean score of visualization was 2.88 ± 0.35 out of 3 and all surgeons gave score 3 out of 3 for ease of manipulation.

The patients were monitored for 1 to 2 mon for the presence of any side effects that may have been associated with the heartworm removal procedure; no evidence of specific adverse effects was identified in any of the eight patients. Follow-up echocardiographic examination were done within 2 d after the heartworm extraction, and in five cases no detection of any suspected heartworm structures in the MPA, right atrium, right ventricle, or caudal vena cava was confirmed. Additional follow-up echocardiography was done in four patients except case 1, 2, 3, and 7. The last postoperative follow-up echocardiography was done after 2 mon, 2 mon, 1 mon, and 1 wk in case 4, 5, 6, and 8, respectively. MPA dilation remained similar in all four patients. In case 4 and 5, peak velocity of tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary regurgitation reduced after 2 mon. In case 6 and 8, peak velocity of both tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary regurgitation both remained similar in last echocardiographic examination. Postoperative adulticidal therapy injecting melarsomine (Immiticide; Merial, Germany) was started within 1 mon after heartworm removal. All clinical signs patients presented recovered after the heartworm removal. Lethargy and anorexia resolved within 1 wk in every patient. Dyspnea resolved within 1 wk in six dogs and within 2 wk in two dogs. Peritoneal effusion and pleural effusion due to right-sided heart failure took a relatively longer time to resolve. Peritoneal effusion and pleural effusion decreased significantly within 3 wk and 4 wk, respectively.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: In this study, efficacy of improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush in dogs with caval syndrome was evaluated. Various of devices that can be used through venotomy for the treatment of caval syndrome have been devised for decades. These devices are all designed to be less invasive than surgical removal through thoracotomy and shorten the procedure time to minimize exposure to anesthesia [811131619]. The alligator forceps need ample space because the jaw has to be opened to grasp the heartworms, thus it is difficult to use in small animals [1113]. In addition, forceps can cause damage to the cardiovascular tissue due to sharp tip of the jaw [1112]. Basket or snare devices cause relatively less damage than forcep devices due to the use intravascular catheter guide, and can be applied to small animals but it is relatively expensive [11121314]. Forceps, basket and snare devices can also crush heartworms during the procedure, causing acute shock reactions [11121318]. Recently, several simplified low-cost brush-type devices have been introduced [1112162021]. Despite their simplicity, they have the advantage of being less invasive to the cardiovascular tissue than conventional forceps, baskets, and snare devices [12162021]. However, depending on the device, the knot can still irritate the cardiac tissue and vessels [12], can be difficult to manipulate because of the absence of a control knob [20], and there are difficulty distinguishing the device under fluoroscopy.

The improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush used in this study compensates for the limitations described in the previous paragraph. This device is made of a polyvinyl chloride tube with polyamide or polyglyconate threads located in the front region. The threads’ knot is made in the inner surface of the tube to minimize potential tissue damage compared to a knot outside of the tube. At the distal tip of the tube, a plastic stopper was installed as the handle and location marker. Metallic material is inserted in the front tip of the tube so that the location of the tube can be specified under fluoroscopy.

To the best of our knowledge, to date no studies have evaluated the efficacy of brush-type devices, although several studies have reported the efficacy of flexible alligator forceps [17222324]. Furthermore, a study on the efficacy and comparison of basket devices and tripod grasping forceps has been reported [1314]. There was high efficacy of heartworm removal using improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush in this study since no obvious adverse effects related to the procedure were observed. All eight patients received proper adulticidal therapy after the removal and treatment terminated safely with no significant complications throughout the treatment. Many complications can occur related to heartworm removal. The mortality rate of dogs that underwent heartworm removal by venotomy is 29% according to previous study [8]. Coughing due to an immune-mediated reaction to crushed heartworms, pulmonary thromboembolism, hemorrhage, aspiration pneumonia or death have been reported [81214]. Additionally, Death due to hypotension, cardiac arrest during the procedure also have been reported [8]. In this study no deaths occurred during or after heartworm removal, and no patients had worsening respiratory signs. In the laboratory tests, D-dimer levels, which can be an indicator for thromboembolism, were checked in five patients and blood level did not elevated after the removal. No other significant abnormal changes or symptoms after the procedure were observed and all clinical signs resolved in all of the patients in this study. Therefore, the use of this device can be considered safe.

The improved heartworm extraction brush devised in this study had a high score for visualization and manipulation in the qualitative evaluation. The mean visualization score and manipulation score were 2.88 ± 0.35 out of 3 and 3 out of 3, respectively. The small radiopaque metal material at the tip of the tube was considered to have contributed to good device visualization under fluoroscopy. Many brush-type brushes are made of relatively radiolucent tubes and threads compared to conventional forceps, snare, basket devices, which are distinctly visible under fluoroscopy [11121416192021]. The device used in this study improved this limitation and was expected to have contributed to the successful operations. At the other end of the brush, a movable plastic stopper was attached to act as a handle and indicator for the length of the tube insertion. When capturing the heartworms after inserting the device into the heart, several rotations are required to catch the heartworms between the threads. This stopper helps the surgeon to hold the tube more securely.

The mean procedure time until the last worm was extracted was 38.63 ± 43.28 min. Previous studies had mean procedure times of 30 ± 7.6, 24.5 ± 6.2, and 42.3 ± 15.7 min for alligator forceps, basket device, and tripod forceps, respectively [1422]. In this study, the mean total procedure time was also calculated, 72.63 ± 51.36 min. This was a relatively longer time in comparison to previous studies [1422]. Several more attempts were made after the last heartworm was extracted to ensure the removal of as many heartworms as possible. Therefore, some of the last attempts may have been unnecessary in some cases. These attempts were expected to lengthen the total procedure time because the mean procedure time until the last heartworm was removed was much shorter than the total procedure time. The shortest total procedure time was 30 min and the longest total procedure time was 195 min. The time differences may have been larger because the criteria for additional attempts differed depending on the surgeon as this was a multicenter study, and multiple surgeons performed the removals. In addition, the removal procedure is safer and faster with skilled hands [13], and differences in proficiency between surgeons may also have affected the procedure times. Despite the longer procedure times, there were no deaths or side effects related to the procedure.

In case 7, a Labrador Retriever with a body weight of 28 kg, the procedure time was more than three times longer than the average time, and more than 20 attempts were needed. This could be attributed to several factors. First, as explained in the previous paragraph, surgeon proficiency in performing the procedure might have had an influence. Second, because locating the removal device in the right ventricle is difficult in a large breed dog or the right atrium is dilated [13], removal may not have been easy because of the patient’s size and right atrium dilation confirmed by ultrasound. For other reasons, a brush with a thread size of 5 cm was used; an inappropriate brush size selection could have been the reason for prolongation. The size of the brush was smaller compared to the size of the heart lumen. In large breed patients, it may be helpful to use a brush with longer threads. After the procedure, this dog recovered well, and no significant adverse effects were observed, even though the number of attempts was high.

In previous studies using flexible alligator forceps, the removal rates ranged from 89.3% to 93.6% [17222324]. In this study, due to the limitations of not being able to perform autopsy after the removal, accurate removal rate calculation was difficult. Many reported devices use ultrasonography to confirm the absence of remaining heartworms to determine when to finish the removal procedure [14182021]. In this study, we also used ultrasonography to detect remaining heartworms after the removal. In addition, transesophageal echocardiography has recently been reported to improve the heartworm removal efficacy [25]. Since live heartworms in the heart cannot be detected by fluoroscopy [14], using conventional echocardiography or transesophageal echocardiography would help increase the removal rate and improve the efficacy of the heartworm removal.

Postoperatively, the clinical signs associated with heartworm infection and caval syndrome progressively improved. During echocardiographic follow-up, severe tricuspid regurgitation progressively normalized. In all patients, complications related to heartworm infection, such as pulmonary inflammation, thromboembolism, Wolbachia spp.-associated complications [14], oral medication with prednisolone as an anti-inflammatory agent, clopidogrel as an anticoagulant agent, and doxycycline were treated. Additionally, sildenafil was administered to all eight dogs to treat pulmonary hypertension. Postoperative adulticidal therapy is essential because of the potential for the remaining heartworms in the pulmonary arteries [12]; therefore, first immiticide therapy was also started in all patients within 1 mon after heartworm extraction with melarsomine in an intramuscular injection.

This study has several limitations. This study had a small sample size. Since there were no comparative evaluations between other brush type devices, and quantitative evaluation was performed for the visualization and manipulation per each surgeon’s discretion, there was weak objectivity in this study. In addition, due to the limitations of a retrospective study, no criteria were set up for brush size selection; therefore, brush size was selected according to the surgeons’ judgement. Furthermore, in the process of confirming the remaining heartworms and cardiovascular damage, only echocardiography was performed and no autopsy was performed. Therefore, there is possibility undetected residual heartworms or tissue damage may not have been identified.

No significant side effects associated with heartworm removal using an improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush were detected. However, the device we used can have potential problems as an extension of these limitations above. First, there is still has possibility of damaging heartworms during extraction. Forceps, baskets, and snare devices can potentially crush or transect heartworms and lead to shock reaction [1112131819]. There is lack of information about brush-type device compared to these conventional devices. However, heartworms may be crushed because multiple heartworms come out tangled with the threads. Additionally, it has been reported that horsehair brush can only retrieve small number of worms [21]. Therefore, extraction can be difficult when multiple heartworms are tangled within the heart chamber.

In conclusion, an improved transvenous heartworm extraction brush was devised that was used efficiently for heartworm removal in dogs with caval syndrome. This brush can compensate for the limitations of the previous brush-type devices. Additionally, the brush is relatively less expensive than conventional forceps, basket- or snare-type devices. Despite its low cost, the efficacy of heartworm extraction was confirmed to be high, and no significant side effects were detected. Therefore, its use could be practical in the future, not only in local hospitals, but also for shelter dogs that have a high risk of exposure to Dirofilaria immitis.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: We would like to thank Able Implant & Instrument for their assistance with making the instrument.

fn.p: Conflict of Interest: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

caption.p: Results of quantitative and qualitative evaluation categories

caption.title: Supplementary Table 2
caption.p: Number of removed heartworms on each trial
